name: Quiz Design Psychometrics — Reliability and Validity
core_claim: This is not a single construct but the measurement-quality discipline that governs whether ANY personality-based onboarding quiz produces a usable signal. The core claim of psychometrics is that an observed quiz response = true trait + measurement error, and that the proportion of variance that is true-trait (reliability) bounds how much the score can predict anything downstream (validity): the validity coefficient between a quiz score and an outcome cannot exceed the square root of the product of the two reliabilities (attenuation by unreliability). For a low-friction onboarding quiz this is the load-bearing fact: a 5-10 item quiz that is unreliable will silently cap the recommendation engine's ability to use personality at all, no matter how good the recommender is. Three measurement frameworks are relevant: (1) Classical Test Theory (CTT) — reliability via Cronbach's alpha / test-retest, and the Spearman-Brown prophecy formula which quantifies exactly how reliability rises with test length; (2) Item Response Theory (IRT) — models each item's discrimination and difficulty/location independently of the sample, enabling item-level information functions; (3) Computerized Adaptive Testing (CAT) — uses IRT to pick the next item that maximises information at the respondent's current estimated trait level, achieving full-length precision with far fewer items. The link to viewing enjoyment is indirect: psychometrics does not predict media preference, it determines whether the personality estimate fed into the recommender is signal or noise. Its 'matters because' is purely instrumental — it sets the ceiling on every other personality-based field in this research set.
predictive_validity_for_media: Psychometric quality has no direct media-preference effect size of its own; its empirical role is to cap the effect sizes of the constructs it measures. The mechanism is attenuation: if true Openness correlates with complex-content preference at rho=0.30, but the 6-item quiz has reliability 0.60, the OBSERVED correlation is attenuated to ~0.30 * sqrt(0.60) = ~0.23; at reliability 0.40 (typical TIPI Agreeableness alpha) it drops to ~0.19. So the practically observable predictive validity of a short personality quiz is the construct's true validity degraded by the square root of the quiz's reliability. Key quantitative findings from the literature: (a) Two items is the floor at which an internal-reliability estimate can even be computed; reliability rises steeply from 2 items through the single digits, then flattens (Spearman-Brown), so the marginal reliability gain from item 8 to item 10 is small while item 1 to item 3 is large. (b) Single-item-per-trait measures (SIMP) have test-retest reliabilities ranging ~0.35-0.78 (one-month) and as low as 0.35-0.77 at three months — usable as a rough prior but heavily attenuating. (c) IRT-built short forms can match or beat the reliability of the full-length parent scale by selecting maximally informative items (e.g. EPQ-R short form built via IRT; NEO-FFI IRT analyses). (d) CAT delivers near-full precision with a fraction of items — a documented 16-item CAT matched a 74-item full scale and out-classified a 22-item static brief form. Distinguishing the four prediction targets is downstream of the measured construct, not of psychometrics per se — see the per-construct files (sensation-seeking, need-for-cognition, Big Five) for genre/thematic/narrative/completion specifics. Psychometrics only tells you how much of each of those is recoverable at a given item count. [uncertain]
instrument_design: This is the central field. Design levers for a 5-10 item quiz: (1) ITEMS PER TRAIT vs TRAITS COVERED. Reliability is a property of items-per-trait, not total items. With a 10-item budget you face a hard tradeoff: 5 traits x 2 items (broad but each trait near the reliability floor, TIPI-style alphas ~0.40-0.68) OR 2-3 traits x 3-4 items (narrow but each trait reliable enough to attenuate less). For a recommendation prior, prefer FEWER traits measured WELL over more traits measured noisily — drop domains, not items-per-domain. (2) RESPONSE FORMAT. Likert agreement (e.g. 5- or 7-point) is fastest and preserves cross-person comparability (normative scoring), which the two-person use case REQUIRES; but it carries acquiescence and social-desirability bias. Forced-choice / ipsative formats reduce faking and acquiescence but produce within-person rank scores that break cross-person comparison unless modelled with Thurstonian IRT — recent work also questions whether forced-choice actually beats Likert on faking resistance once social desirability is controlled. (3) ADAPTIVE vs FIXED. True CAT needs a calibrated item bank (hundreds of pre-tested items with IRT parameters) and is overkill for onboarding; a lightweight 'pseudo-adaptive' branch (ask 2 screening items, then 2-3 follow-ups only on the trait showing the most extreme response) captures some CAT efficiency without an item bank. MINIMUM VIABLE under-2-min, 6-10 item version: 8-10 Likert items covering 2-4 high-yield traits at 2-3 items each, balanced for keying (include reverse-scored items to net out acquiescence), single screen, slider or segmented control. Completion ~60-90s. Do NOT use a single item per trait if that trait will drive ranking — single-item retest reliability (0.35-0.78) attenuates too hard. Use the Spearman-Brown formula at design time to set a reliability target (aim >=0.70 per trait used for ranking; accept ~0.50-0.65 for a soft seed).
cold_start_applicability: Highly relevant as a meta-constraint on every cold-start personality approach. With zero viewing history, the quiz is the entire signal, so its reliability directly bounds cold-start quality — there is no behavioural data to dilute measurement error against. The key cold-start insight from psychometrics: signal quality at t=0 is reliability-capped, but it does not need to be high to be useful as a PRIOR that is rapidly overwritten by real interactions. A reliability-0.55 trait estimate is a poor clinical instrument but an acceptable Bayesian prior whose influence decays as the recommender accumulates implicit feedback. For cross-medium inference, psychometrics adds a warning: the SAME quiz score is being asked to predict TV, film, AND anime preference, so any unreliability is shared across all three predictions (correlated error), not averaged out. Practical cold-start design rule: weight the personality prior in inverse proportion to its measured reliability (a noisier trait gets less initial weight and decays faster). Also relevant: at t=0 you cannot estimate the user's individual response style (acquiescence), so build the quiz to be robust to it (balanced keying) rather than correcting for it post-hoc.
group_compatibility_use: Psychometrics imposes two specific constraints on the two-person joint use case that are easy to miss. (1) NORMATIVE SCORING IS REQUIRED. To combine or compare two partners' trait estimates (average, distance, least-misery), the scores must be on a common interpersonally-comparable scale. Likert/normative formats satisfy this; ipsative/forced-choice formats do NOT — ipsative scores are within-person ranks, so 'Partner A's Openness > Partner B's Openness' is uninterpretable from raw ipsative data. This makes Likert the safer default for HelpME2C despite its bias costs. (2) ERROR COMPOUNDS IN AGGREGATION. When you combine two noisy estimates, the error of the combined quantity depends on both. A trait DISTANCE between partners (used as a compatibility signal) has error that is roughly the sum of both individual error variances — so a partner-distance signal computed from two short noisy quizzes is substantially noisier than either individual score. Implication: distance/compatibility signals from a 6-10 item quiz are the LEAST reliable derived quantity and should be used coarsely (e.g. 'close / moderate / far' buckets) rather than as a continuous input. Least-misery aggregation is also reliability-sensitive: it keys off the more extreme member, and extreme scores are exactly where short-scale measurement error is largest (conditional error inflates at the range extremes for few discretely-scored items per IRT), so a least-misery rule can fire on noise. Recommendation: bucket aggregation inputs, A/B-test the rule, and treat partner-distance as a weak signal until enough joint-viewing feedback accrues. Direct empirical evidence linking quiz reliability to joint-satisfaction prediction was not located; this is reasoned from measurement theory. [uncertain]
quiz_ux_implications: Psychometrics gives concrete, actionable UX rules. (1) BIASES THAT APPLY: acquiescence (yea-saying) is the dominant threat in short Likert scales and is best handled by balanced keying (include reverse-scored items) rather than by lengthening the quiz; social-desirability/faking is LOW risk here because there is no incentive to fake taste for a recreational recommender (unlike hiring, where forced-choice anti-faking matters) — so the heavy machinery of forced-choice is not justified and would cost cross-person comparability. (2) DISGUISE vs DIRECT: direct elicitation ('rate how much you agree') is psychometrically cleanest and acceptable given low faking stakes; gamification/disguise improves engagement and can reduce reactance but introduces uncalibrated measurement error (you no longer know the item's IRT parameters), so any gamified item should ideally be validated against a known scale before its score is trusted. (3) FRICTION vs RELIABILITY is the core UX tension this whole research item exists to resolve: every item removed lowers reliability per Spearman-Brown, every item added raises drop-off. The resolution: spend the item budget on the 2-4 traits that actually move recommendations, present them on one screen, use a continuous control (slider/segmented) to extract more information per item than a 5-point radio, and frame as 'help us learn your taste' not 'a personality test' (reduces reactance AND softens the privacy posture). (4) Avoid double-barrelled or ambiguous items at short length — with few items, one bad item disproportionately damages the trait estimate (no redundancy to absorb it).
cross_cultural_validity: Psychometrics surfaces the single hardest cross-cultural problem for short quizzes: MEASUREMENT INVARIANCE CANNOT BE ESTABLISHED AT VERY SHORT LENGTHS. To claim a Korean user's Openness score means the same thing as a German user's (a prerequisite for comparing or combining them — scalar invariance), you must run configural/metric/scalar invariance tests, which require enough items per factor to identify the measurement model. A 2-item-per-trait scale (TIPI-style) generally CANNOT support formal invariance testing — there are too few indicators to identify the latent factor across groups — so you literally cannot verify comparability, which is exactly what the two-person multilingual use case depends on. Established findings: invariant short scales CAN be built but must be ENGINEERED for it — items are selected specifically for cross-cultural invariance (e.g. Ant Colony Optimization and automated item-selection procedures that pick item subsets passing invariance constraints; the Big Five Personality Trait Short Questionnaire achieved invariance across Spanish-speaking countries this way). Response-style differences (acquiescence, extreme-responding) vary systematically by culture and contaminate short Likert scales most; balanced keying partially corrects this. Practical rule for HelpME2C: if cross-language profile comparison matters at launch, you cannot just translate a 2-item-per-trait quiz and trust it — either (a) use a 3-4 item-per-trait subset selected/validated for invariance, or (b) keep personality strictly within-language (compare partners only when same UI language) until invariance is demonstrated. Translation alone is insufficient; invariance is an empirical claim that requires data. [uncertain]
privacy_consent_implications: Psychometric data is personal data under GDPR. The reliability/validity framing adds a specific privacy nuance: the MORE reliable and validated the instrument, the stronger the inference it licenses, and the closer the stored score edges toward GDPR Article 9 special-category territory (a well-validated Neuroticism estimate is a more defensible mental-health proxy than a noisy one). Conversely, deliberately low-fidelity 'taste seed' framing both reduces friction and reduces the strength of sensitive inference — a privacy argument for storing the derived recommendation prior rather than clinical-grade trait scores. Requirements: explicit, freely-given consent at quiz time with a clear purpose statement; data minimisation (store the seed, not raw clinical-labelled traits; never expose 'neuroticism' in user-facing fields); deletable on DSAR and hard-deleted on account deletion per ADR-0012. This is stop-and-ask territory per CLAUDE.md section 4 (privacy-control flows). A defensible posture: collect transiently, derive the prior, and store the prior with the option to discard raw responses.
relationship_to_theme_vocabulary: Psychometrics does not map to theme slugs directly — the measured constructs do (see Big Five, sensation-seeking, need-for-cognition files for slug mappings). What psychometrics contributes is a CONFIDENCE WEIGHTING over those mappings. Each construct-to-theme link (e.g. high Openness -> nonlinear-storytelling, earned-ambiguity, slow-burn, character-study, magical-realism, cosmic-horror; high sensation-seeking -> chase-and-escape, survival-and-endurance, existential-horror, post-apocalyptic-survival; high need-for-cognition -> mystery-investigation, whodunit, ethical-dilemma) should be applied with strength proportional to the reliability of the trait that drives it. Practically: the theme weights derived from a high-reliability trait estimate (e.g. a 4-item Openness subscale, alpha ~0.75) should be applied at near-full strength, while weights from a 2-item or single-item trait (alpha ~0.45) should be shrunk toward zero (regularisation toward the population prior). This means the SAME theme mapping table is used across all personality files, but psychometrics governs the multiplier on each row. Concrete design output: store a per-trait reliability estimate alongside each trait score, and multiply that trait's contribution to theme weights by a function of its reliability (e.g. reliability itself, or reliability^0.5 to match the attenuation formula). This prevents a noisy trait from confidently pushing a user toward, say, cosmic-horror on the basis of measurement error. [uncertain]
implementation_recommendation: INCLUDE as a cross-cutting design discipline, not as a quiz of its own. Concrete recommendations: (1) Set an explicit per-trait reliability TARGET at design time using Spearman-Brown — aim >=0.70 for any trait that directly drives ranking, accept 0.50-0.65 for soft-prior traits, and refuse single-item-per-trait for any trait used in ranking. (2) Spend a 6-10 item budget on 2-4 high-yield traits at 2-3 items each (fewer traits, measured well) rather than spreading thin across all five Big Five domains. (3) Use balanced-keying Likert (reverse-scored items included) on a single screen with a continuous control; direct elicitation, framed as 'learn your taste', NOT gamified/disguised at launch (gamification de-calibrates the items). (4) Store a reliability estimate per trait and use it to (a) weight the personality prior inversely to noise, (b) decay it faster when noisier, and (c) shrink theme-weight contributions of noisy traits toward the population prior. (5) For the two-person case, require normative (Likert) scoring for comparability, bucket partner-distance into coarse bands rather than continuous, and A/B-test averaging vs least-misery. (6) Defer true CAT — it needs a calibrated item bank (hundreds of items) that is unjustifiable at MVP; revisit only if a large validated item pool becomes available. (7) Defer cross-language profile COMPARISON until measurement invariance is demonstrated on the chosen item subset; until then keep personality within-language. DEPENDENCIES: this item is a governor over every other personality file (Big Five short forms, sensation-seeking, need-for-cognition, HEXACO, etc.) — it should be read as the methodological contract those instruments must satisfy, not as a standalone feature. North-Star trace: 'reliability-aware quiz design ensures the personality seed fed into both partners' first joint recommendation is signal rather than noise, so the recommender is not silently capped by measurement error before any viewing history exists' — traceable.
key_sources: Spearman-Brown prediction formula (test length vs reliability) — Wikipedia (free): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spearman%E2%80%93Brown_prediction_formula ; Evans R-blog practical note incl. 2-item reliability caveat (free): https://www.psyctc.org/Rblog/posts/2021-04-09-spearman-brown-formula/ . IRT primer — Revelle, 'The New Psychometrics: Item Response Theory' (free PDF): https://www.personality-project.org/r/book/Chapter8.pdf ; IRT overview (free): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Item_response_theory . IRT-built short personality form — EPQ-R short form via IRT (free PMC): https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6190847/ ; NEO-FFI IRT analysis (free PMC): https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3417236/ . Computerized adaptive testing efficiency (16-item CAT ~ 74-item full scale) — CAT for schizotypal PD (free PMC): https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7868333/ ; CAT-PD development: https://www.acsu.buffalo.edu/~robertsj/catpd.pdf (free PDF). Single-item personality reliability meta-analysis (SIMP retest 0.35-0.78) — De Vries, Realo & Allik / meta-analytic guidelines (free PMC): https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4332286/ . Forced-choice vs Likert response-bias control (free PMC): https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6803422/ ; forced-choice does not always beat Likert on faking (free PMC): https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11095325/ . Cross-cultural invariant short-scale construction (Ant Colony Optimization) — Jankowsky et al. 2020, European Journal of Personality (paywalled abstract): https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/per.2260 ; Big Five short questionnaire invariance across Spanish-speaking countries (free PMC): https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6917272/ ; automated invariant item-selection (Hogrefe, paywalled): https://econtent.hogrefe.com/doi/abs/10.1027/1614-2241/a000155 .
